segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a young man, characterized by his short black hair, round glasses, and light pink button-up shirt, seated in a modern, cozy room with ambient blue-purple lighting, speaking animatedly into a professional microphone. He uses expressive hand gestures and wide-eyed smiles as he declares, "First off, and I just want to get this out of the way, I'm not playing Mahjong for the anime waifus, okay? Mahjong Soul is not a game about anime waifus." He challenges viewers to "just take a look at their official ad." The scene then cuts to a vibrant, festive animated sequence from the game 'Mahjong Soul,' showcasing steamed buns with cute faces and the text 'Festive Aroma' in glowing script, accompanied by the cheerful background music 'Splendid Dreams' from the 'A Feast for Nian Event.' The video returns to the man, who, with a slight chuckle, concedes, "Okay, there are some anime waifus in the game." He then shifts to a more serious tone, stating his love for Riichi Mahjong and explaining why, revealing, "I've got low self-esteem." He passionately describes the game's risky yet valuable hands, the need to "believe in yourself and fight for a chance to win," and the fun of playing "without regrets." He announces his intention to create "a tutorial for the very basics of Riichi Mahjong" for his YouTube videos and Twitch audience, explaining the rules of the game. As he continues speaking with energy, occasionally pointing, a split-screen appears, displaying a collage titled 'Riichi Mahjong' featuring anime-style characters and mahjong tiles, including 'Nadeshiko' with 'Big Three Dragons,' while the man appears in a small inset window, still smiling and reacting to the content. He concludes the segment by saying, "PowerPoint."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a split-screen 'Riichi Mahjong' collage, showcasing anime characters and tiles, as the Young Man, in a small inset, reacts with expressive gestures and a lighthearted 'it's PowerPoint.' He then explains that he primarily plays Riichi Mahjong on 'Mahjong Soul,' a game whose vibrant title screen, featuring anime-style female characters and cherry blossoms, is displayed, noting it's 'available for free in web browsers, iOS, or Android,' with optional cosmetic purchases. The scene transitions to a chalkboard-style 'Mahjong Tiles' tutorial screen, where a chibi character, Ichihime, introduces the 34 unique tiles. The Young Man, gesturing towards the screen, elaborates on the tile types, explaining there are three main suits—character tiles (Man), circles (Pin), and bamboo (Sou)—each numbered one through nine, and honor tiles, which include the four winds (East, South, West, North) and the three dragons (White, Green, Red). He emphasizes that these tutorial images are from the game itself and suggests viewers complete it. He concludes by stating that a full Mahjong set contains 136 tiles, with four copies of each, before the view shifts to an overhead 3D-rendered mahjong table with tiles arranged for gameplay, as he begins to explain further.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens on a mahjong game tutorial screen, titled 'How to win a hand: Triplets and Sequences,' presented on a chalkboard-style interface. A chibi character, Ichihime, with brown hair, cat ears, and a red-and-white outfit, stands to the left, visually explaining the rules. The Young Man, visible in a small inset window, actively engages, nodding and pointing as he articulates examples of sequences, stating, "789 of the character suit and 567 of the bamboo suit." He further clarifies that "9 is the highest of any suit, we can't go above that, like a sequence can't be 891." He enthusiastically declares, "If we can combine those concepts together, we can win." The tutorial then transitions to a 'Winning' screen, illustrating that a winning hand requires '4 Sequences or Triplets + 1 Pair.' The Young Man continues to explain, "So, in order to win, you need four sequences or triplets and one pair." He elaborates on the flexibility of combinations, noting, "The wording for four sequences or triplets can be a little confusing, so let me explain. You can have any combination of those. For example, a winning hand could be two sequences and two triplets. Another winning hand could have four sequences and no triplets." He emphasizes, "but in both of those examples, you still need a pair, in order to complete your winning hand." The screen then shifts to a quiz, asking, 'Which tile does the hand need for winning?' displaying a hand of tiles and three multiple-choice options. The Young Man, gesturing and speaking into his microphone, reacts with enthusiasm, saying, "Okay, great. So, the tutorial has some like little test questions... So let's try to answer this together. So which tile does the hand need for winning? Uh, let's take a look at it. So, first off, we got a pair, it seems, of the two of characters. That seems pretty good. That seems..." as he analyzes the options. The camera briefly zooms in on the hand of tiles, highlighting the arrangement and the question mark placeholder. Throughout, the Young Man's room maintains a consistent dark, grid-patterned background with ambient blue-purple lighting, featuring shelves, decorative items, and a keyboard.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens on a digital mahjong tutorial screen, presented on a teal chalkboard-style interface, posing the question 'Which tile does the hand need for winning?' The hand displays various mahjong tiles, including red 'Man', black 'Pin', green 'Sou', and a green dragon. Below, three options are visible: A. a 2-Pin tile, B. a 3-Sou tile, and C. an East Wind tile. On the left, the chibi character Ichihime, with brown hair, cat ears, and a red-and-white outfit, provides the instruction: 'Make sure your hand becomes 4 Sequences or Triplets + 1 Pair nyaa!' In the bottom right, a Young Man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, speaks into a microphone, actively analyzing the mahjong hand. He articulates his thought process, identifying a pair and sequences, stating, "looks like we've got our pair taken care of," and then pointing out, "we have the four, the five, and the six of characters... That seems like a sequence to me." He continues to break down the hand, noting a "three of circles and a four of circles here," and humorously concluding, "It's not enough for a sequence, so this is looking kind of sus." He then identifies bamboo sequences, summarizing, "in total we have our pair, we have one sequence, two sequences, three sequences, and we need to finish this one." After posing, "So, what do we need to win?" he confidently declares, "It's gonna be the two of circles. That's the tile that we need to finish this hand." He then selects option A (2-Pin), which prompts Ichihime's visual feedback: 'Try another one-nya!' The hand on the screen immediately resets to a new configuration, now showing a sequence of 1-9 Pin tiles, three 1-Flower tiles, and a 2-Sou tile, with the same three options. The Young Man, his expression shifting from thoughtful to slightly amused, continues his analysis, reading the new puzzle title, "Which tile does the hand need for winning?" and remarking, "Uh, okay, this one's a little trickier, so let's let's go through it," as he begins to identify sequences in the new hand, such as "the one, two, three of circles... That's one sequence." The background maintains a dark, grid-patterned digital interface with ambient blue-purple lighting throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a 'Calling Tiles: Pon' tutorial screen from 'Mahjong Soul,' displaying a 3D mahjong table with 'AI(Easy)' avatars and a hand of tiles. A blue circle highlights a 9 of characters tile, indicating a 'Pon' call with 'Pon' or 'Skip' options and a countdown. The chibi character Ichihime, with brown hair, cat ears, red eyes, and a red-and-white traditional outfit, appears on the left, cheerfully explaining the rule. Simultaneously, a Young Man, with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, is seen in a small inset window, speaking into a microphone with expressive gestures in a cozy, blue-purple lit room, explaining how to "reveal the two that you have and then take their tile to complete your triplet set," assuring viewers an example will be shown later. The scene then transitions to the 'Calling Tiles: Chii' tutorial, where the Young Man announces, "Next, let's talk about Chii, which is how we can complete sequences." The screen now shows a green circle highlighting a 4 of bamboo tile, with the 'Chii' option and a countdown. Ichihime continues to explain the 'Chii' rule, emphasizing its use for completing a sequence with the last player's discarded tile. The Young Man elaborates on an example, stating, "in this example, we have the six of circles and the eight of circles and the player to our left has discarded the seven of circles, which is exactly what we need. So, we can Chii their tile, reveal our six and eight, and use their seven to complete a sequence." He cautions that 'Chii' can only be used for sequences and only when the player to the left discards the needed tile, not the player across or to the right. He then playfully asks, "Uh, you song, is any of this gonna be on the prelim? Yes, it's gonna be on the prelim. Quiz time," as the tutorial presents a quiz question: 'Which of the following hands can Chii the 6 of bamboo from the last player?' with three multiple-choice options (A, B, C) displayed below. Ichihime encourages the player with the text 'If you answer the question correctly, you can have my snack-nya!' The Young Man continues to engage with the content, gesturing and speaking into the microphone, maintaining his expressive demeanor while beginning to analyze the quiz, noting, "Chii means we're completing a sequence here. And so this one's starting to look good."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens on a chalkboard-style Mahjong tutorial screen, displaying the quiz 'Which of the following Sequences is a Yaku?' with options A (three East Wind tiles), B (three South Wind tiles), and C (three West Wind tiles). Ichihime, a chibi-style anime character with cat ears, provides visual hints about Yaku referring to Prevalent Wind, Seat Wind, and Dragon Tiles, while a score of 'East 1' and '25000' points is visible. The Young Man, in an inset window, actively analyzes the question, stating in an engaging voice, "East one round right now. So, the East is looking pretty good as the prevalent wind," and then, "We're also sitting in the East seat. The scene transitions to a new quiz, 'Which tile should you discard if you want to achieve Tanyao?', showing a hand of tiles and options A (1 of Bamboo), B (7 of Bamboo), and C (3 of Circles). Ichihime visually explains that Tanyao disallows Terminal or Honor tiles. The Young Man continues his analysis, asking, "Which of these tiles should you discard if you want to achieve Tanyao, aka all Simps?" He clarifies, "if you want all Simps, you can't have any ones, nines, or honor tiles," which include wind and dragon tiles. He then reviews the tile options, noting, "Pair twos, those seem great. Four, five, six of characters, that seems great. Three, four, that seems great." Upon seeing the '1 of Bamboo' option, he exclaims humorously, "One, two, three, excuse me, in this economy? Excuse me? Okay, that's a little sus." He concludes, "the only sus tile that you have in here is the one that you want to discard, and that's going to be the one of..." as the audio fades, all against a dark, grid-patterned digital background with ambient blue-purple lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The video displays a chalkboard-style mahjong game tutorial screen, titled 'Simple Yaku: Riichi,' which explains the Riichi rule. The Young Man, seated in a cozy, blue-purple lit room with shelves and decorative items, animatedly explains the rule in an enthusiastic voice, stating, "Alright! Let's talk about the Yaku that's in the name of Riichi Mahjong, Riichi," playfully calling it "the one that makes you feel like an anime protagonist." He elaborates that a player can declare Riichi if they achieve Tenpai without having called Chi, Pon, or Kan, a condition also displayed on the screen above example mahjong tiles. He clarifies that the player must be "one tile away from having a winning hand," referencing the displayed tile examples (2-6 Characters, 3-7 Bamboo, West, East, West, West, and a 3 Circles) by noting, "All we need to finish it is either a two of circles or a five of circles." He describes the declaration action as discarding a tile sideways, calling it "very anime, very cool," which signals to others, "hey, I'm one tile away from winning, so be careful." He concludes by explaining that victory comes if an opponent deals the needed tile or if the player draws it themselves on their turn, all while the chibi-style anime character Ichihime, with brown hair, cat ears, red eyes, and a red-and-white traditional outfit, observes with a cheerful expression from the left side of the screen, which features a dark, grid-patterned background with faint mahjong tile motifs and a 'Skip' button.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a young man, wearing round glasses and a light pink button-up shirt, speaking into a microphone from a cozy, modern room with blue-purple lighting, as he introduces the 'Pinfu' yaku on a detailed Mahjong 'Yaku Overview' tutorial screen. He explains that Pinfu requires four sequences and one non-Yakuhai pair, emphasizing that winning tiles must be from both sides of a sequence, stating, "Again, very confusing." He then simplifies, clarifying, "Pinfu means you need four sequences. No triplets in this one, okay?" The screen zooms in on tile examples, showing sequences like 1-2-3 Man, 4-5-6 Pin, and 7-8-9 Sou, as he elaborates on the 'double-sided wait' condition, illustrating with examples like waiting on the five or eight of bamboo, contrasting it with a single-sided wait. He summarizes the Pinfu requirements, then reads the 'Menzenchin Only' condition, humorously remarking that it "sounds like the name of a character you'd get a blessing from in Elden Ring," before explaining it means no 'pon' or 'chi' calls. Throughout, he uses expressive hand gestures, pointing and nodding, maintaining an animated and informative demeanor, as the interface displays various yaku categories and their conditions, including 'Pure Double Sequence' and 'Half Flush,' before he transitions to discuss the latter.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment features a detailed tutorial on Mahjong yaku (scoring patterns) within the game interface, specifically focusing on the 'Yaku Overview' screen. The screen displays various yaku categories such as 'Pinfu' and 'Pure Double Sequence,' each with tile examples and descriptions. The 'Pinfu' rule is highlighted, explaining that a hand must consist of four sequences and one non-Yakuhai pair, with potential winning tiles being from both sides of a sequence, and is marked as 'Menzenchin Only.' The 'Pure Double Sequence' rule requires two identical sequences and is also labeled 'Menzenchin Only.' The interface includes tabs for different han levels (1 Han, 2 Han, 3 Han, etc.) at the top. The Young Man, visible in a small inset window, speaks into a microphone with expressive gestures, pointing and nodding as he explains the rules. The background behind him features shelves, decorative items, and ambient blue-purple lighting, creating a cozy, modern atmosphere. The screen occasionally zooms in on specific tile arrangements to emphasize the examples, such as sequences of 1-2-3 Man, 4-5-6 Pin, and 7-8-9 Sou, along with pairs and other combinations. The tutorial transitions to show additional yaku like 'Half Flush' and 'Local Yaku: Pure Triple Chow,' each with their own tile examples and conditions, including penalties like '-1 Han after making a call.' The Young Man continues to engage with the content, using hand gestures to illustrate points, maintaining an animated and informative demeanor throughout the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens on a dark blue 'Yaku Overview' tutorial interface from a digital mahjong game, displaying tabs for various han levels and cycling through yaku like 'Half Flush' and 'Local Yaku: Pure Triple Chow.' A Young Man, with short black hair and round glasses, wearing a light pink button-up shirt, appears in an inset in the bottom right, speaking into a microphone with expressive gestures and facial expressions from a cozy, modern room with blue-purple lighting. He explains the 'Half Flush' yaku, detailing how to win with tiles from one of the three Number Tiles and Honor Tiles, clarifying, "Basically, you can only have one suit of tiles and then you can finish the rest of your hand with honor tiles, however you'd like." He points out the visual example of tiles in the character suit alongside winds and dragons, describing it as "a very, very common hand" while humorously admitting, "I haven't been there in a while. No, just kidding." The tutorial screen notes a '-1 Han after making a call' penalty. The Young Man then guides the viewer as the interface navigates to the '6 Han' tab, revealing the 'Full Flush' yaku. He explains that 'Full Flush' requires winning with "only one suit," emphasizing, "this is way hard, but it's very, very valuable." He briefly touches on how yaku value is determined by 'han' before the segment concludes with him reiterating the 'Full Flush' requirement of "entirely one suit."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment features a tutorial interface from a digital mahjong game, specifically focusing on yaku (scoring patterns) within the game. The screen displays a dark blue interface titled 'Yaku Overview' with tabs for different han levels (1 Han, 2 Han, 3 Han, 6 Han, Mangan, Yakuman, Double Yakuman, Draw). The content cycles through various yaku, starting with 'Half Flush' and 'Local Yaku: Pure Triple Chow,' each accompanied by tile examples and descriptions. For instance, 'Half Flush' requires winning with tiles from only one of the three Number Tiles (Man, Pin, or Sou) and Honor Tiles (Wind and Dragon), while 'Pure Triple Chow' involves three identical sequences. The interface also notes a penalty: '-1 Han after making a call.' The user navigates through the tutorial, eventually selecting the '6 Han' tab, which reveals the 'Full Flush' yaku—winning with tiles from only one suit (Man, Pin, or Sou). Throughout, the Young Man appears in a small inset window in the bottom right, speaking into a microphone with expressive hand gestures and facial expressions, reacting to the tutorial content. He is in a cozy, modern room with ambient blue-purple lighting, shelves, and decorative items, including a keyboard and framed artwork. The background of the tutorial screen features a faint grid pattern with subtle mahjong tile motifs, and the game’s interface includes a 'Tutorial' button on the left and a close ('X') button in the top right corner.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a tutorial screen titled 'Yaku Overview,' displaying the 'Full Flush' yaku with mahjong tiles and a note about losing 1 Han after a call. A Young Man, visible in an inset, speaks into a microphone with expressive gestures, explaining the yaku rules, stating, "No dragons, just one suit... you can do this with a character suit... or the circle suit or the bamboo suit." He then announces, "Okay, let's do an example game," as the scene transitions to the game's vibrant main menu, featuring cherry blossoms, a traditional Japanese building, and the character Yu_song. The Young Man continues to speak, guiding the viewer through the menu options, explaining, "this screen is what you're going to see basically... when you log in and create an account... for the first time and let me show you how to set up a game versus AI at first so you don't have to jump into ranked match immediately." He then clicks on 'Friendly Match' with an audible click, instructing, "You're going to want to create create room." On the 'Create Room' screen, he configures a 4-Player vs AI match, advising to "crank that up to 300" for thinking time (300+0s), and to leave advanced settings "disabled." After clicking 'Create' (another audible click), the room is set up with Yu_song as the host, showing a 'Ready' status. As an animated cutscene of characters playing table tennis loads, the Young Man comments, "Wholesome husbandos and waifus shown," before exclaiming, "All right, let's get into it." A distinct game sound effect plays as the 'Match Start' screen appears, leading into the gameplay on a 3D-rendered mahjong table, displaying the player's hand, AI opponents, and a central score of 'East 1' and 25000 points. The Young Man remains engaged, reacting to the game's progression, concluding with "Great."

segments.integrated_caption: The video segment opens with a live gameplay session of a digital mahjong game, viewed from an overhead perspective on a blue table. The interface displays four player positions, with avatars labeled 'AI(Easy)' and 'Yu_song', the latter being the player whose hand of tiles (including 3, 7, 1 circles; 2, 4, 5, 6, 7 bamboo; South, Green, Red Dragon) is visible at the bottom. The central score display shows 'East 1' with 25000 points and a multiplier of x61, which decreases to x54. The player, Yu_song, whose voice narrates the gameplay, explains that an opponent discarded the five of circles, which is exactly what he needs to call 'Chii'. He decides to "showcase how we do it" and calls 'Chii'. Visually, a chibi-style character with brown hair and cat ears, Ichihime, briefly appears as an animation to confirm the action, aligning with Yu_song's narration that "your character pops up" and "you reveal your characters." On-screen text confirms, '*You reveal your tiles!'. Yu_song explains that everyone at the table can now see his revealed tiles. He then exclaims, "all simps, baby, all simps ride or die," connecting to the on-screen text '*Tanyao is just another name for all simps!'. He discards a tile, and AI opponents take their turns. Yu_song, seen in a small inset window in his cozy, blue-purple lit room, adjusts his glasses and continues to speak into his microphone, commenting on a 'Southwind' tile ("you hate to see it") but reiterating "we're going for all simps here." He elaborates on real Mahjong strategy, emphasizing not to be "tunnel visioned on a specific Yaku" but to "go with the flow." He then explains why he could call Chii, stating the player on his left discarded the three of circles, and he holds the one and two, anticipating the viewer's thought of being "one step closer to making a complete hand" before warning them to "watch out 'cause."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with the Young Man, visible in an inset window, providing clear and analytical commentary on a live 'Mahjong Soul' gameplay session. He explains his strategy to achieve 'Tanyao,' noting that calling 'Chii' on a tile would lock in a 1-2-3 sequence and prevent a 'Yaku,' stating, "We're going for all simps here, okay?" He rejects a 'three of circles' tile, deciding to "call skip here" as the digital mahjong interface displays 'Yu_song's evolving hand with various circles and bamboo tiles. The central score display updates from a x54 multiplier to x53. He reacts positively to drawing a bamboo tile, exclaiming, "Great. Awesome. Drawing another bamboo tile. Let's let's keep cutting our honors." As the game progresses, the multiplier drops to x50. He observes the AI opponents' discards, reacting with an excited "Wow! Wow, wow, wow!" when an 'AI(Easy)' player discards a 'five of bamboo,' which he interprets as "feeding us delicious tiles." He then analyzes his own hand, noting "five, six, six, seven of bamboo," and humorously contemplates calling 'Chii' to "get some brunch with a mimosa," before explaining that doing so would complete a 5-6-7 sequence, which he then executes, all while gesturing expressively in his softly lit room.

The player discards a tile, and the game interface updates accordingly, with the 'Chii' and 'Skip' buttons visible. The atmosphere is focused and analytical, with the Young Man providing commentary on the strategic implications of each move.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a continuous gameplay session of a digital mahjong game, viewed from the perspective of the player 'Yu_song' at the bottom of the screen. The interface displays a blue mahjong table with four player positions, featuring 'AI(Easy)' avatars and 'Yu_song's' avatar. The central score reads 'East 1' with 25000 points, and a multiplier counter, starting at x50, steadily decreases. In a small inset window, a Young Man with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt is visible, speaking into a microphone with expressive facial movements and hand gestures, reacting to the gameplay from a room with shelves and blue-purple lighting. He immediately engages in strategic commentary, noting, "we still have five six. So, if this player discards a four or a seven, we can still make a sequence just like that." He then decides, "all Sims, we're tunneling, let's cut this red dragon," as 'Yu_song' discards a '中' (Red Dragon) tile from their hand, which includes '三萬', '七萬', '一萬', '四萬', and various bamboo and circle tiles. As 'Yu_song' draws a '一萬', then a '七筒', and an 'AI(Easy)' opponent discards a '一萬', the Young Man exclaims, "Wow, seven. Okay, that's new. That's new," reacting to the drawn '七筒', adding, "I'm kinda liking the look of that seven." He continues strategizing, stating, "in order to finish this hand, we're going to need to cut this one eventually," and decides, "let's do it now because we can't have ones, nines, or any of these honor tiles." As 'Yu_song' draws a '四萬' and another 'AI(Easy)' opponent discards a '七筒', the Young Man excitedly declares, "Four! Woo! Okay, this is looking great. This is looking great 'cause that goes right next to our three of characters." He highlights the progress, "now we can make another sequence based off of that. And we're one step closer to finishing our hand," and adds, "we even have a pair of the seven of characters. This is looking phenomenal." The multiplier counter decreases to x37, and the number '293' on the right updates, as the Young Man concludes, "Let's cut this two of circles for now."
segments.visual_original_caption: The video segment shows a continuous gameplay session of a digital mahjong game, specifically from the perspective of the player 'Yu_song' at the bottom of the screen. The interface displays a blue mahjong table with four player positions, each marked with an avatar labeled 'AI(Easy)' except for the player's own avatar, 'Yu_song,' at the bottom. The central score display reads 'East 1' with a base score of 25000, and a multiplier counter (starting at x50) decreases as the round progresses. segments.integrated_caption: The video captures a young man, with short black hair, round glasses, and a light pink button-up shirt, deeply engrossed in a digital mahjong 'Friendly Match - 4-Player (vs AI)'. He speaks into a microphone from his cozy, blue-purple lit room, explaining, "now it's the new round. It's still the East round," and that he's "sitting in the East seat." The game interface, featuring a blue table, displays his avatar 'Yu_song' at the bottom and three 'AI(Easy)' opponents. The central score reads 'East 2' with a multiplier decreasing from 'x69' to 'x61' and a score of 29900, while a static '293' is visible on the right. He outlines his strategy, stating, "Let's go for a Riichi hand," but notes, "we can't call Chi or Pon unfortunately," adding, "we kind of have to just play with ourselves." As the game progresses, AI opponents discard '北' (North), '西' (West), and '東' (East) tiles into the growing discard pile. The young man, adjusting his glasses and gesturing expressively, explains his tile management: "I'm just going to cut some of these honor tiles to begin with because... honor tiles, you can only make triplets of them, right? You can't make sequences."
